coredata.dc:title: Convex-nanorods of α-Fe2O3/CQDs heterojunction photoanode synthesized by a facile hydrothermal method for highly efficient water oxidation
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2017 Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Longzhu
Liu, Changhai
Qiu, Yangyang
Mitsuzak, Naotoshi
Chen, Zhidong
coredata.dc:description: Morphology controlling and surface modification of semiconductors is the key for efficient photoelectrochemical (PEC) water splitting systems. This work provides a new strategy for achieving morphology control and heterojunction construction simultaneously by one-step hydrothermal method. The α-Fe2O3/CQDs heterojunction photoanode with convex-nanorods morphology is successfully prepared by hydrothermal method in CQDs (Carbon Quantum Dots) aqueous contained iron precursor followed by low temperature annealing treatment. Compared with bare hematite photoanode, the α-Fe2O3/CQDs photoanode has 8.5 time higher photocurrent density (at 1.23 V vs. RHE) of 0.35 mA cm−2 and a negative shift of onset potential about 300 mV. The enhanced photoelectrochemical response is attributed to the convex-nanorods which benefit higher absorbance of light and the formed α-Fe2O3/CQDs heterojunction, which can efficiently enhance the electron-hole separation and reduce the surface charge recombination. The morphology and properties of the sample were characterized with scanning electron microscopy (SEM), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), Fouriertrans form infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), UV–vis spectra, X-ray diffractometry (XRD), X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS), and photoelectrical measurements.
coredata.dcterms:subject: α-Fe2O3/CQDs
Heterojunction
Convex-nanorods
CQDs
Water oxidation

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Top-view and (b) cross-section SEM images of the α-Fe2O3 photoanode. (c) Top-view and (d) cross-section SEM images of the α-Fe2O3/CQDs photoanode. (Inset of (c) shows high magnification top-view SEM image, Inset of (d) shows top view of 45° tilted SEM image).
(a) (b) TEM images of α-Fe2O3 photoanode. (c) (d) TEM images of α-Fe2O3/CQDs photoanode.

(a) Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy of FeOOH and FeOOH/CQDs. (b) X-Ray diffraction patterns and (c) higher resolution curves of Fe of α-Fe2O3 and α-Fe2O3/CQDs. (d) Schematic illustration for the structure of the α-Fe2O3 and α-Fe2O3/CQDs photoanode.
(a) UV–vis absorbance spectra of the photoanodes. (b) (αhν)1/2-hν plot of the photoanodes.

(a) Photocurrent densities-potential curves in the dark (dashed lines) and under simulated solar light irradiation (solid lines) of the fabricated photoanodes (AM 1.5 global, 1 sun (100 mW cm-2)). (b) Mott−Schottky plots of α-Fe2O3 and α-Fe2O3/CQDs photoanodes measured in the dark under 1 kHz. (c) Photocurrent densities-potential curves under light illumination with various amounts of CQDs (the amounts of CQDs was controlled by the ratio of synthesized CQDs solution to deionized water = 0, 1:1, 2:1 and 1:2). (d) Stability of α-Fe2O3 and α-Fe2O3/CQDs photoanodes under light illumination at 1.23 V (vs. RHE).
The schematic illustrations of the band bending corresponding to the measured absolute OCVdark and OCVlight values of the α-Fe2O3 and the α-Fe2O3/CQDs photoanodes.

(a) Open circuit potential (OCP) curves of α-Fe2O3 and α-Fe2O3/CQDs photoanodes under chopped light. (b) Nyquist plots of the α-Fe2O3 and α-Fe2O3/CQDs photoanodes. (c) Corresponding equivalent circuit model and fitting impedance parameter values. (Inset shows magnification Nyquist plots of high frequency).

head.ce:title: Convex-nanorods of α-Fe2O3/CQDs heterojunction photoanode synthesized by a facile hydrothermal method for highly efficient water oxidation

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Jiangsu Key Laboratory of Material Surface Science and Technology, Changzhou University, Changzhou, 213164, Jiangsu, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering
Jiangsu Key Laboratory of Material Surface Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical and Materials Engineering, Changzhou Vocational Institute of Engineering, Changzhou, 213164, Jiangsu, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemical and Materials Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Morphology controlling and surface modification of semiconductors is the key for efficient photoelectrochemical (PEC) water splitting systems. This work provides a new strategy for achieving morphology control and heterojunction construction simultaneously by one-step hydrothermal method. The α-Fe2O3/CQDs heterojunction photoanode with convex-nanorods morphology is successfully prepared by hydrothermal method in CQDs (Carbon Quantum Dots) aqueous contained iron precursor followed by low temperature annealing treatment. Compared with bare hematite photoanode, the α-Fe2O3/CQDs photoanode has 8.5 time higher photocurrent density (at 1.23 V vs. RHE) of 0.35 mA cm−2 and a negative shift of onset potential about 300 mV. The enhanced photoelectrochemical response is attributed to the convex-nanorods which benefit higher absorbance of light and the formed α-Fe2O3/CQDs heterojunction, which can efficiently enhance the electron-hole separation and reduce the surface charge recombination. The morphology and properties of the sample were characterized with scanning electron microscopy (SEM), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), Fouriertrans form infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), UV–vis spectra, X-ray diffractometry (XRD), X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS), and photoelectrical measurements.

ce:section.ce:para: Photoelectrochemical (PEC) approach was studied as one of the most promising technology for solar energy conversion as the continuously increasing energy demand. The photoactive semiconductor electrodes of photoelectrochemical cells can generate photo-excited carriers under light irradiation and transfer minority carriers to water to perform one half of the water splitting reaction. Among these, oxidation (O2 evolution, OER) occurs at the anode, and reduction (H2 evolution, HER) occurs at the cathode [1]. Iron is the fourth most common elements in the earth's crust. The hematite (α-Fe2O3) as the most common form of iron oxide become a particularly attractive photoanode material for photoelectrochemical water splitting, due to its small band gap (Eg ≈ 2.0 eV) that can absorb visible light and its excellent chemical stability in aqueous solutions [2–4]. However, the performance of hematite photoanodes is limited by low absorption coefficient [5,6], low carrier conduction [7], short hole diffusion lengths (2–20 nm) [8,9] and the slow kinetics of the oxygen evolution reaction [10]. Since Hardee and Bard [11] first turned to iron oxide as a material for water photolysis in 1976, researchers have made many fruitful strategies to improve the efficiency of hematite, including doping, surface modification and morphology controlling [12]. Recently, dopants including Si [13], Ti [14,15], Pt [16], P [17], Mo and Cr [18], and Zr [19] have been shown to significantly improve the PEC performance by enhancing conductivity due to the increased donor concentrations and the lattice strain. At the same time, there has been much effort to decrease the overpotential of photocurrent with surface modification by Al2O3 [20], fluoride [21], rGQDs [22], CoPi [23], and heterojunction [24,25]. Furthermore, Nanostructured α-Fe2O3 materials have been synthesized by numerous approaches to achieve nanorods, nanowires, nanotubes, nanoplate, dendritic nanostructures [12] and porous nanostructure [26]. In which, the dendritic bare α-Fe2O3 nanostructure photoanode has the best PEC response up to now due to the dendritic nanostructure minimized the distance photogenerated holes have to diffuse to reach the interface of α-Fe2O3 and electrolyte and simultaneously allowed efficient light absorption [27].

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) refer to nanosized carbon materials with at least one dimension less than 10 nm in size, which are applied in solar-to-energy conversion due to their ease of synthesis, low cost, excellent performance such as excellent electrical conductivity and visible light absorption, and non-toxicity. As one material-design approach, heterostructures constructed from CQDs and semiconductors with appropriate band alignment can facilitate interfacial charge-transfer processes [28].

Sivula et al. [3] conclude that it is possible to approach the performance of an ideal hematite by combining the two strategies of decreasing the overpotential with surface modification and increasing photocurrent by morphology control. Thus, we developed a novel and simple strategy of hydrothermal method for simultaneously completing the surface modification and morphology controlling of α-Fe2O3 photoanode, which has an obviously enhanced activity as photoanode for water oxidation. The morphology, crystal structure and PEC properties of the photoanodes were characterized, the results show that α-Fe2O3/CQDs heterojunction photoanode with convex-nanorods morphology has 8.5 times higher photocurrent density (at 1.23 V vs. RHE) of 0.35 mA cm−2 and a negative shift of onset potential about 300 mV.

The α-Fe2O3 coating was prepared on F-doped SnO2 glass (FTO) by hydrothermal method according to previous work [29] followed by low temperature annealing treatment. Initially, the FTO substrates were cleaned and dried. Secondly, 0.61 g FeCl3 and 1.27 g NaNO3 were dissolved in 15 ml deionized water. Then the solution was transferred into a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave with a capacity of 80 mL and 3 pieces of FTO substrates (1 × 4 cm2) were tipsily placed. The autoclave was sealed and heated at 95 °C for five hours to grow FeOOH films, and then the as-synthesized FeOOH was successively annealed at 500 °C in pure argon atmosphere for two hours to convert to hematite (as α-Fe2O3). The α-Fe2O3/CQDs heterojunction coating was prepared with the same process of α-Fe2O3 coating, excepting that the deionized water was replaced by carbon quantum dots (CQDs) aqueous solution. The CQDs was prepared by a hydrothermal method as reported by Li et al. [30]. In short, ammoniumcitrate (16 mmol) and ethylenediamine (4 mL) were dissolved in water (20 mL), and then the solution was transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave (80 mL) and heated at 200 °C for five hours.

The surface functional groups were determined by Fouriertrans form infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, Nicolet IN10, Thermo Scientific). The crystal structure was analyzed by using Rigaku D powder X-ray diffraction (XRD, max 2500 PC, Rigaku). The UV–vis absorbance spectroscopy (UV 2450, Shimadzu) was used to probe the optical properties. The morphology were characterized by scanning electron microscopy (SEM, JSM-6360LA, JEOL) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM-2100, JEOL). The films' surface was characterized by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, ESCALab 250Xi, Thermo Scientific).

The PEC properties were investigated using a standard three-electrode electrochemical workstation (CHI 760E) with the fabricated samples as working electrode, a platinum foil as the counter electrode and an Ag/AgCl (sat. KCl, E = 0.1976V vs. RHE.) as the reference electrode. The measured potentials versus Ag/AgCl were all converted to the reversible hydrogen electrode scale according to the Nernst equation, VRHE=VAg/AgCl+0.059 pH + 0.1976. The 1 M NaOH (pH = 13.6) was used as electrolyte, and the 300 W Xe lamp equipped with an AM 1.5 filter was used to irradiate the simulated solar light as AM 1.5 global and 1 sun (100 mW cm−2) was calibrated with full spectrum optical power meter. The photocurrent was measured by linear sweep voltammetry from 0.4 to 1.7 V (vs. RHE) with a scan rate of 0.03 V s−1. The Mott−Schottky (MS), Open circuit potential (OCP) and Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) were carried out employing the same electrochemical device except that a VersaSTAT 3 potentiostat was used. The MS plots were measured in the dark at 1 kHz, the OCP transient tested was carried out under chopped light, and the EIS measurements were performed at OCP with the frequency range being adjusted between 100 kHz and 0.1 Hz at amplitude frequency 10 mV under light irradiation.

In order to investigate the physicochemical property of the synthesized CQDs, The fluorescent emission spectra measurement, fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) and thermogravimetric analysis (TG) were carried out. As shown in Fig. S1a, the emission wavelengths of synthesized CQDs are excitation-independent, which is agreement with ref. [30] as having few surface defects and narrow size distribution. Fig. S1b indicated that the CQDs have rich functional groups, such as –OH, –NH2, –C–H, –C=O–NH–, C–O and C–N. To evaluate the reduction degree of the CQDs in the nanocomposite coatings, TG analysis has been carried out at a heating rate of 2 °C min−1 in argon atmosphere as shown in Fig. S1c. Obviously, CQDs is not thermally stable due to the decomposition of oxygen-containing functional groups such as –C=O–NH– and C–O, and which reach to 80% of the total weight of CQDs. The rest of the CQDs are free with oxygen-containing functional groups.

The microstructure of the fabricated α-Fe2O3 and α-Fe2O3/CQDs photoanodes are characterized by SEM and TEM as seen in Figs. 1 and 2
. From Fig. 1a and b, uniform α-Fe2O3 nanorods are grown on FTO substrate with the length around 500 nm, and the nanorods morphology can be further confirmed by TEM image in Fig. 2a. The diameter of the α-Fe2O3 photoanode is determined by TEM (Fig. 2b) as 40 nm. As seen in Fig. 1c, the α-Fe2O3/CQDs photoanode has isolate island appearance exposing the internal surface of the electrode film to the electrolyte. The cross-section SEM image (Fig. 1d) of the α-Fe2O3/CQDs photoanode shows that the photoanode has nanorods morphology with the length around 750 nm and the 45° tilted SEM image shows the nanorods morphology more clearly. In order to further reveal the morphology of the α-Fe2O3/CQDs photoanode, the TEM images were shown in Fig. 2c and d. Combining the SEM and TEM images, it can be concluded that the α-Fe2O3/CQDs photoanode has nanorods morphology with the diameter of the major nanorods was around 100 nm and with fine structure of sharp convex as less than 20 nm, and which is designated as convex-nanorods. HRTEM image of α-Fe2O3/CQDs photoanode was shown in Fig. S2, it can be seen that the CQDs have a graphitic structure with the typical lattice spacing of 0.340 nm [30], and the lattice fringe of 0.27 nm is agrees well with the {110} lattice spacing of α-Fe2O3.

Fig. 3 a shows the FTIR spectroscopy of the fabricated FeOOH and FeOOH/CQDs obtained from CQDs solution. It can be seen that the FeOOH/CQDs electrode is inorganic–organic hybrid nanostructures. The characteristic peaks at 3400–3500 cm−1 can be identified from both samples, which are attributed to vibrations of the -OH functional groups in FeOOH. The enhanced transmission peak at 3400–3500 cm−1 of FeOOH/CQDs can be assigned to the stretching vibrations of –NH2 from the raw materials ammoniumcitrate and ethylenediamine for making CQDs, and the peak at 817 cm−1 may be assigned to the N-H wag vibration. The typical peak at 1651 cm−1 and 1404 cm−1 is associated with the bending vibrations of –C=O–NH– and vibration of C–O, respectively. It can be concluded that CQDs solution introduce many oxygen-containing groups to the FeOOH/CQDs electrode. As seen in Fig. S3, the static contact angle measurements clearly demonstrate that the FeOOH/CQDs electrode has much better hydrophilic property compared to FeOOH electrode. According to the result of FTIR, the main reason is that there are many organic hydrophilic groups on the surface of the FeOOH/CQDs electrode. After annealing treatment, the α-Fe2O3/CQDs electrode has little better hydrophilic property compared to α-Fe2O3 due to the decomposition of oxygen-containing functional groups as TG analysis.

The XRD patterns of the fabricated electrodes can be indexed to the characteristic peaks of α-Fe2O3 (JCPDS NO: 33-0664) (Fig. 3b). The exclusive strong (110) diffraction peak and much weaker peaks indicate both of α-Fe2O3 nanorods and α-Fe2O3/CQDs convex-nanorods are highly oriented in the [110] direction on the substrate, which is consonance with the results of SEM and TEM (Figs. 1 and 2 and Fig. S2). However, the α-Fe2O3/CQDs photoanode with stronger (110) diffraction peak indicates higher crystallinity compared with the α-Fe2O3 photoanode.

XPS was carried out to ascertain the component of the α-Fe2O3/CQDs electrode and the interaction between α-Fe2O3 and CQDs. The full-scale XPS spectra (Fig. S4) shows that the α-Fe2O3/CQDs electrode consists of Fe, O, C and little N elements. The Fe and O signals are from α-Fe2O3, C and N peaks are attributed to the CQDs. The little nitrogen contained in the CQDs is due to the raw materials ammoniumcitrate and ethylenediamine for making CQDs. As shown in higher resolution curves of Fe 2p Fig. 3c, the binding energy (BE) of Fe 2p3/2 peaks with a predominant intensity at 710.5 eV correspond to the typical trivalent Fe species. In the α-Fe2O3/CQDs electrode, there is a small peak at lower BE of Fe 2p3/2 corresponding to the divalent Fe. Yan et al. [31] reported that the presence of Fe2+ in the α-Fe2O3 lattice was achieved by annealing the NRs in a lack of oxygen atmosphere, e.g. in argon, in which the peaks of Fe3O4 were observed in the XRD patterns for the samples annealed in argon. While the peaks of Fe3O4 could not found in the XRD patterns of fabricated samples (Fig. 3b), which were in consonance with the result of Liu et al. [32] as annealing the FeOOH nanorods to 450 °C in an argon atmosphere led to the complete reduction of FeOOH to α-Fe2O3. Thus, it can be concluded that the divalent Fe of the α-Fe2O3/CQDs electrode was caused by CQDs not argon atmosphere. The main reason may be the trivalent Fe atoms in Fe2O3 will get some electrons from CQDs in the hetero-structured electrode due to the surface electron-rich properties of CQDs.

Based on the above results, a possible schematic model for the structure of the α-Fe2O3 and α-Fe2O3/CQDs photoanode is proposed in Fig. 3d, where the α-Fe2O3 sample formed without CQDs solution grows in the [110] direction with nanorods morphology. As the addition of CQDs solution, the CQDs bind strongly onto the surface of the Fe nuclei through functional organic groups during the process of hydrothermal synthesis of the α-Fe2O3/FeOOH sample. The coated CQDs make the crystal growing along the [110] direction with a more elongated grain and limited tilt angles. After annealing treatment, oxygen-containing functional groups of CQDs decompose and form convex-nanorods CQDs coated α-Fe2O3 with isolate island appearance. It can be concluded that the CQDs adsorbed on the α-Fe2O3/CQDs photoanode lead to fast growth in the [110] direction with limited tilt angles and eventually forming the convex-nanorods morphology.

As shown in Fig. 4 a, the UV–Vis absorbance spectra of the fabricated photoanodes have a clear absorption edge around 600 nm, corresponding to the band gap energy of the typical hematite electrodes. Apparently, the notable higher absorbance was found for α-Fe2O3/CQDs photoanode compared with α-Fe2O3 photoanode during full spectrum range. The enhanced absorbance of α-Fe2O3/CQDs photoanode can be attributed to the increased length of nanorods and the convex-nanorods which cause diffuse reflection phenomenon by light multiple scattering according to Mie theory as the scattered wavelength is dependent on the size of the light scattering center [33]. At the same time, the mixed CQDs with smaller band gap may contribute to the enhanced absorbance of long wavelength [34]. The Tauc plot (Fig. 4b) shows an indirect optical band gap of 2.15 eV for α-Fe2O3 photoanode and 2.05 eV for α-Fe2O3/CQDs photoanode, further modified by the mixed CQDs.

To investigate the PEC performance of the as-prepared photoanodes with different morphology, the photocurrent response under simulated solar light irradiation has been measured. Fig. 5 a shows the photocurrent density-potential curves of the fabricated samples. It can be seen that the α-Fe2O3/CQDs photoanode shows an enhanced photocurrent density of 0.35 mA cm−2 (at 1.23 V), which is almost 8.5 times higher than that of the α-Fe2O3 photoanode as 0.04 mA cm−2. For investigating the effect of amount of CQDs on the photocurrent response, the Photocurrent densities-potential curves under light illumination with various amounts of CQDs were shown in Fig. 5c. It can be seen that the photocurrent density is improved as the amount of CQDs increasing but decreased when the concentration of CQDs is higher. Moreover, the long term stability tests are shown in Fig. 5d, and all the fabricated electrodes show a good stability at this potential during measuring time. Furthermore, the method reported by Le Formal et al. [35] was used to quantitatively analyze the PEC performance of the fabricated photoelectrodes. In which, the onset potential is defined as the potential at which the change in photocurrent as a function of potential first exceeds 0.2 mA cm−2 V−1. As shown in Fig. S5, by plotting the first-order derivative of the photocurrent density against the voltage for the fabricated photoanodes, the α-Fe2O3/CQDs photoanode has onset potential of 0.9 V and 300 mV negative shift compared with α-Fe2O3 photoanode of 1.2 V. The mechanism may be the photoexcitation in the CQDs generates electron/hole pairs in the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) and highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) levels, respectively. Then, the excited electrons are transferred from the LUMO level to the conduction band of the α-Fe2O3 and the photocatalytic activity is improved.

To clarify the origin of the different photocurrent response and electron transport mechanism of the fabricated photoanode, the Mott-Schottky (MS) has been measured and are shown in Fig. 5b. In addition, the donor density (ND) and flat-band potential (Efb) of the fabricated photoanodes are inserted in Fig. 5b, which are deduced from the slope and intercept of the MS plots according to the MS equation (in supporting information). For the MS analysis is derived from a planar electrode model, it is some reasonable to calculate the carrier density for comparison purpose [36]. The positive slope of MS plot indicates that both of the fabricated photoanodes are n-type semiconductor with electrons as the majority carriers. The α-Fe2O3/CQDs photoanode has lower Efb = 0.35 V and higher ND = 1.03 × 1020 cm−3 compared with the α-Fe2O3 photoanode which has Efb = 0.57 V and ND = 4.82 × 1019 cm−3, in which the increased ND indicates the enhanced electron density. The Efb values can be directly related to the measured onset potentials of photocurrent since flat band potential is theoretically equal to onset potential of photocurrent. Thus the Efb of the α-Fe2O3/CQDs photoanode with negative potential shift is in a good agreement with the measured onset potential of photocurrent in Fig. 5a. In the α-Fe2O3/CQDs photoanode, some electrons of CQDs were transferred to the conduction band of the α-Fe2O3 according to the result of XPS. In this charge equilibration process, the Fermi level of the α-Fe2O3/CQDs photoanode goes up, which leads to negative potential shift of Efb and onset potential of photocurrent.

To further discuss the enhanced PEC performances of the α-Fe2O3/CQDs photoanode, the transient open circuit potential (OCP) (equal to the difference of open circuit voltage upon light illumination (OCVlight) and in dark (OCVdark)) of the fabricated photoanodes were carried out and the results were shown in Fig. 7 a. In which, the OCVdark reflects the upward band bending of the photoanode equilibrium with the electrolyte in dark, and the OCVlight is determined by the negatively Fermi level shift in the photoanode materials under illumination. The degree of the band bending is determined by the built-in potential in the photoanode/electrolyte junction, the minority carrier accumulation and the charge recombination. From Fig. 7a, it can be seen that the fabricated electrodes show a negative increase in voltage under light irradiation, indicating the photogenerated electrons are injected from the electrodes into the FTO substrate, and the photoanode acts as an n-type semiconductor material which is in a good agreement with the MS result [37]. It is also noted that the OCP of the α-Fe2O3/CQDs photoanode as 0.178 V is larger than that of the α-Fe2O3 photoanode as 0.104 V, indicating that the α-Fe2O3/CQDs photoanode possesses preferable photoelectric conversion ability. From Fig. 6, it can be concluded that the α-Fe2O3/CQDs photoanode with an enlarged band bending of 0.19 eV represents the enhanced electron-hole separation and leads to the larger OCP [38]. We can assign this enhanced electron-hole separation to the formation of the α-Fe2O3/CQDs heterojunction which promotes formation of a thicker space charge layer and facilitates charge separation, and then decreases the overpotential for oxygen evolution [39].

To further probe the quality of the photoanode/electrolyte junction, the lifetime of the mobile photogenerated carriers can be expressed as a function of OCP as the equation developed by Zaban et al. [40].
(1)
τ = −
k B
T
e
(
d O C P
d t
)
− 1
where τ is the carrier lifetime, k
B
T is the thermal energy, and e is the positive element charge, the carrier lifetime can be quantified for comparison of the charge recombination rate in the junction. According to the equation, the carrier lifetime can be deduced from the slope of the OCP-t plots (Fig. S6). In the α-Fe2O3/CQDs photoanode, the carrier lifetime is 2 s compared to that of 23 s for the α-Fe2O3 photoanode at the transient when the illumination is stopped at the open-circuit condition. This indicates that the α-Fe2O3/CQDs photoanode has increased charge recombination when illumination is stopped. Consequently, the enhanced charge separation is expected in the same junction under illumination, which suggests the α-Fe2O3/CQDs photoanode has improved photoanode/electrolyte junction quality. The main reason is that the CQDs can rapidly collect photogenerated electron-hole pairs owing to the excellent electrical nature and heterojunction. According to the static contact angle measurement (Fig. S2), the one reason of improved junction quality of α-Fe2O3/CQDs/electrolyte is the formed hydroxyl-rich on the α-Fe2O3/CQDs surface which prevent the surface trapped electrons to be in directly contact with the electrolyte and therefore reduce the surface charge recombination.

To explore the charge transport dynamics in the electrodes, the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) are measured at OCP and illumination, which is the same condition as the photocurrent measurements. Fig. 7b shows the Nyquist impedance of the fabricated photoanodes analyzed in a frequency range of 0.1–105 Hz and the corresponding equivalent circuit model fitted from the obtained EIS data by program Zsimpwin, where the dots in the plots represent the experimental data and the solid lines represent the result of fitting. The EIS equivalent circuit model includes two sub-circuit which containing a resistance and a capacitance (RC) in parallel circuits. The fitted impedance parameter values of the resistances (R) and constant phase elements (CPE) are listed in Fig. 7c. The small semicircle at high frequency (R1/CPE1) reflects the recombination of charges in bulk. The large semicircle at low-frequency (R2/CPE2) represents the larger interface resistance between n-type semiconductor electrode and electrolyte, which is the most difficult process of an entire photoelectrochemical reaction system due to slow charge transfer kinetics caused by energy mismatching between the acceptor d orbitals of hematite and donor p orbitals of the O2-OH- redox couple and surface states [41–43]. The fitting results indicate that the α-Fe2O3/CQDs photoanode exhibits lower resistance R1 for electrons recombining with holes and the charge transfer resistance R2 from the surface state of the electrode. The α-Fe2O3/CQDs photoanode with lower resistance R1 indicates decreased electron-hole recombination attributed to the excellent electrical nature of CQDs and the heterojunction of α-Fe2O3/CQDs which coincides with the results of OCP. Furthermore, the small value of R2 of α-Fe2O3/CQDs results in faster hole transfer at the surface, which relates to the highly hydrophilic nature and the CQDs surface passivation corresponding to the fast OCP-decay behavior in Fig. S6. The value of CPE2 is higher in α-Fe2O3/CQDs photoanode compared with that of α-Fe2O3 photoanode. And the value of CPE2 is related to the amount of accumulated charges at the surface and the surface area. As the fast OCP-decay behavior of α-Fe2O3/CQDs photoanode denote that CQDs reduce the hole accumulation, the higher value of CPE2 of α-Fe2O3/CQDs photoanode can be attributed to the increased surface area due to the formation of convex-nanorods and isolate island appearance.

In this study, one-step hydrothermal method was used for achieving morphology control and heterojunction construction simultaneously. The α-Fe2O3/CQDs photoanode with isolate island appearance and convex-nanorods morphology was prepared in CQDs solution by hydrothermal method followed by 500 °C for 2 h annealed treatment. Compared with α-Fe2O3 photoanode, the α-Fe2O3/CQDs photoanode has 8.5 times higher photocurrent density (at 1.23 V vs. RHE) of 0.35 mA cm−2 and a negative shift of onset potential about 300 mV. The α-Fe2O3/CQDs photoanode with convex-nanorods morphology has higher absorbance of light and smaller band gap. The α-Fe2O3/CQDs heterojunctions in the α-Fe2O3/CQDs photoanode can effectively enhance electron-hole separation by enlarging band bending and reduce the surface charge recombination by surface passivation. Furthermore, the highly hydrophilic nature of α-Fe2O3/CQDs photoanode can be beneficial to the fast OCP-decay behavior for improving junction quality of α-Fe2O3/CQDs/electrolyte.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 51574047), the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (No. BK20160277), Fundamental Research Funds of Changzhou University (No. ZMF16020008), and Fundamental Research Funds of Changzhou Vocational Institute of Engineering (No. 11130100116009).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is the supplementary data related to this article:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.ijhydene.2017.06.078.
